coredata.dc:title: Designing of nucleolus-targeted carbon dots by controlling the carbon and nitrogen sources
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Qianqian
Guan, Zhiwen
Xue, Juanjuan
Zhang, Boye
Zhou, Lan
Jia, Muyue
Si, Si
Sang, Shengbo
coredata.dc:description: Nucleolus selective imaging can reveal the nucleolus structure and characters of cells in different states, and is considered critical in early disease diagnosis and treatment, and in designing targeted delivery systems. In this paper, we analyzed the factors affecting the nucleolus targeting properties of carbon dots by preparing a variety of carbon dots. Only carbon dots with m-phenylenediamine as the carbon source can specifically target the nucleolus. Meanwhile, the nitrogen content in the carbon dots also affects their targeting properties to the nucleolus. The molar ratio of m-phenylenediamine (carbon source) to l-aspartic acid (nitrogen source) was changed to control the nitrogen content in the carbon dots, and the carbon dots had the best nucleolus targeting and imaging effect when the molar ratio was 1:0.5 (carbon: nitrogen). Additionally, binding of carbon dots to RNA in the nucleolus has been demonstrated by digestion experiments, and their cellular internalization is temperature, ATP, and micropinocytosis dependent. Our results provide ideas and methods for the design of nucleolus-targeted carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Carbon source
Nitrogen source
Nucleolus targeting
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell imaging and characterization of nine CDs
Cell imaging and characterization of m-lCDs, o-lCDs and p-lCDs
Cell imaging and characterization of m-lCDs
Specific targeting of RNA by m-lCDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51975400 , 51622507 , 51505324 ), Shanxi Provincial Key Medical Scientific Research Project ( 2021XM12 ), Open Fund from the Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology (Shanxi Medical University ) (No. KLMEC/SXMU202001 ), Shanxi “1331 Project” Key Subjects Construction ( 1331KSC ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent images of MGC-803 cells incubated with different CDs. (A) c-lCDs, (B) c-pCDs, (C) c-aCDs, (D) g-lCDs, (E) g-pCDs, (F) g-aCDs, (G) m-lCDs, (H) m-pCDs, (I) m-aCDs.
(A, B, C): FTIR spectrum of CDs. (D, E, F): Zeta potential of CDs.
The fluorescent images of (A) MGC-803 and (B) GES-1 cells incubated with m-lCDs, o-lCDs, p-lCDs.
XPS survey scan of (A) m-lCDs (B) o-lCDs and (C) p-lCDs. N1s peaks of high resolution XPS spectra of (D) m-lCDs (E) o-lCDs and (F) p-lCDs.

(A) Fluorescence images of SMMC-7721 cells stained with different molar ratios of CDs (150 μg/mL, 2 h). (B) The fluorescence intensity plot of the labeled portion in the molar ratio of 1:2 CDs in A. (C) The fluorescence intensity plot of the labeled portion in the molar ratio of 1:0.5 CDs in A. (D) The fluorescence intensity plot of the labeled portion in the molar ratio of 1:0.125 CDs in A. * represents the nucleolus, & represents the nuclear region, # represents the cytoplasm. (E) Fluorescence intensity of nucleolus region stained with different molar ratios of CDs. (F) Nucleolus to cytoplasm region fluorescence intensity ratio. The molar ratio marked in the figure is carbon:nitrogen.

(A) Presentation of DNase or RNase digestion results. Fluorescence images of SMMC-7721 cells stained by CDs (150 μg/mL, 2 h), SYTO RNASelect (a RNA-specific dye, 1 μM, 15 min) and Hoechst 33342 (a DNA-specific dye, 1 μM, 15 min) after treatment with DNase (25 μg/mL) or RNase (25 μg/mL) for 2 h. Fluorescence spectra of (B) SYTO RNASelect (1 μM) and (C, D, E, F, G, H, I) different molar ratios of CDs (150 μg/mL) in water (Ex = 488 nm). RNA solution (200 μg/mL), and DNA solution (200 μg/mL). The molar ratio marked in the figure is carbon:nitrogen.

(A) Fluorescence images of CDs stained cells under the effect of different inhibitors. (EIPA, micropinocytosis inhibitor), (CPZ, clathrinmediated endocytosis inhibitor), (Nocod., microtubule-dependent transport inhibitor) and (Filipin, caveolin inhibitor) (B) Comparison of fluorescence intensity in Fig. 7A.

head.ce:title: Designing of nucleolus-targeted carbon dots by controlling the carbon and nitrogen sources

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Micro Nano Sensors & Artificial Intelligence Perception

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nucleolus selective imaging can reveal the nucleolus structure and characters of cells in different states, and is considered critical in early disease diagnosis and treatment, and in designing targeted delivery systems. In this paper, we analyzed the factors affecting the nucleolus targeting properties of carbon dots by preparing a variety of carbon dots. Only carbon dots with m-phenylenediamine as the carbon source can specifically target the nucleolus. Meanwhile, the nitrogen content in the carbon dots also affects their targeting properties to the nucleolus. The molar ratio of m-phenylenediamine (carbon source) to l-aspartic acid (nitrogen source) was changed to control the nitrogen content in the carbon dots, and the carbon dots had the best nucleolus targeting and imaging effect when the molar ratio was 1:0.5 (carbon: nitrogen). Additionally, binding of carbon dots to RNA in the nucleolus has been demonstrated by digestion experiments, and their cellular internalization is temperature, ATP, and micropinocytosis dependent. Our results provide ideas and methods for the design of nucleolus-targeted carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Nucleolus is the most obvious structure in the interphase nucleus of eukaryotic cells. The nucleolus plays a crucial role in cell growth, proliferation, aging, protein synthesis, and intracellular signaling [1–3]. Nucleoli are often considered as diagnostic biomarker, which are often hijacked by tumors or viruses, resulting in changes in size, number and function, and these changes are often precursors to a number of malignant diseases [4–8]. Therefore, targeted imaging of the nucleolus is important for early disease diagnosis and treatment in biomedicine. In recent years, various methods have been developed to achieve nucleolus imaging, such as molecular probes [9–11], kernel localization sequences [12] and metallic nanomaterials [13–15]. Until now, only SYTO RNASelect is generally accepted as a nucleolus imaging probe available commercially. However, its high cost and stringent storage conditions, and the fact that it can only stain and image fixed cells, limit its application in live cells. Molecular probes and nucleolus localization sequences have complex structures and cumbersome synthesis methods. The potential cytotoxicity of metallic nanomaterials also hinders their application in bioimaging. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop highly targeted, low toxicity nucleolus imaging probes.

Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of fluorescent quantum dots with a dimensions below 10 nm, have gained much notice due to their interesting photophysical properties, excellent photostability, low toxicity, tunable surface groups and simple synthesis methods, which make them potentially useful in bioimaging applications [16–24]. Nowadays, some nucleolus-targeted CDs have been developed [25–31]. The surface group, charge, particle size, and different elemental doping have a great influence on the cellular internalization and targeting of CDs [32–38]. CDs prepared by m-phenylenediamine and l-cysteine as precursors can specifically stain the nucleolus [25], which probably is contributed to the distinct surface chemistry of CDs, particularly the dedication of the carbon sources. The nitrogen-doped CDs synthesized using citric acid and ethylenediamine have intrinsic nucleolus targeting ability [30], where the doping of nitrogen elements contributes to the nucleolus targeting of the CDs. The CDs potential is altered by adjusting the molar ratio of citric acid and ethylenediamine leading to enhanced nucleolus staining [26]. According to the previous reports, a preliminary summary could be obtained: the CDs can achieve nucleolus targeting through intrinsic functional groups preserved from precursors [39], and the selection of raw materials is important for the targeting effect of CDs [40]. However, the relevance and influence of different carbon sources and nitrogen sources on the organelles targeting performance are not yet uniformly conclusive.

Here, three carbon sources (m-phenylenediamine, citric acid, and glucose) and three nitrogen sources (l-aspartic acid, polyethylenimine (PEI), and ammonia) were chosen as precursors, and nine CDs were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. Among the CDs prepared by different precursors, carbon sources containing benzene ring structures were found to be associated with CDs nucleolus targeting. Then, three phenylenediamine isomers were used as carbon sources to prepare CDs for the further investigation. The imaging results showed that only the CDs with m-phenylenediamine as the carbon source could stain the nucleolus. In addition, by changing the nitrogen source content in the precursor, it was found that the CDs fluorescence gradually diminished with nitrogen content decreasing, and the response to RNA also gradually decreased. Therefore, the m-phenylenediamine was used as a carbon source precursor combined with a certain amount of nitrogen source. The prepared CDs are low cost and easy to synthesize, rich in surface groups for easy modification, and have excellent biocompatibility, high nucleolus targeting and clear nucleolus imaging effect.

Glucose, citric acid, m-phenylenediamine, l-aspartic acid, polyethylenimine (PEI), ammonia, ribonucleic acid (RNA), deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), ethylisopropylamiloride (EIPA), chlorpromazine (Chlorp.), filipin and nocodazole (nocod.) were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group. RNase, DNase, Hoechst 33342, SYTO RNAselect, methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), streptomycin-penicillin, Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and 4% paraformaldehyde were purchased from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) from Gibco, Singapore were procured. The cell culture medium (DMEM/HIGH GLUCOSE) was purchased from Hyclone.

Sample size and morphology characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20). Infrared spectra were detected by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Tensor 27, Bruker Optik, Germany). The fluorescence spectra were detected by fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS-980, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Absorption spectra were measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Metash UV-8000A, 190 nm–1200 nm, China). Fluorescent images and cell viability (OD value) were acquired with Cytation5 Imaging Reader (BioTek, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were performed using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). Zeta potential was measured by Malven Nano ZS zetasizer (Zetasizernano).

Nine CDs (Table 1 ) were synthesized using m-phenylenediamine, citric acid and glucose as carbon sources and l-aspartic acid, PEI and ammonia as nitrogen sources. The molar ratio of carbon source to nitrogen source was consistently 1:0.5 during the preparation process. Take the combination of m-phenylenediamine and l-aspartic acid as an example.

The m-phenylenediamine was used as carbon source, and l-aspartic acid, PEI, ammonia as the nitrogen sources, respectively. Firstly, l-aspartic acid/PEI/ammonia was solubilized in 20 mL of NaOH solution (molar ratio of 1:1) respectively, and m-phenylenediamine (300 mg) was solubilized in 10 mL deionized water, the mixture of the two solutions was then added to the reactor for reaction (160 °C, 10 h). The completely reacted solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and dialyzed with deionized water (MWCO: 1000 Da) for one day.

To further investigate the effect of carbon source, we prepared CDs using m-phenylenediamine, p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine as carbon sources respectively, and l-aspartic acid as nitrogen sources. The preparation method is the same as above.

To study the effect of nitrogen content, the CDs were prepared using m-phenylenediamine as carbon source and l-aspartic acid as nitrogen source, and the molar ratios of carbon to nitrogen were 1:3, 1:2, 1:1, 1:0.5, 1:0.25, 1:0.125 and 1:0.0625. The preparation method is the same as above.

In this study, Human hepatocellular carcinoma cells (SMMC-7721), human liver normal cells (LO2), human gastric cancer cells (MGC-803) and human gastric mucosal cells (GES-1) were used as cell models. Cells were cultured at 5% CO2, 37 °C using DMEM including 10% FBS and 1% streptomycin-penicillin as medium. For cell imaging experiments, the cells were incubated in 24-well plate at a density of 1 × 104 per well for 24 h. After that, the CDs solution was added to each well (150 μg/mL), and the cells were kept incubated for 2 h. Then the cells were washed three times with PBS and fixed for 10 min by adding 500 μL of 4% paraformaldehyde solution per well. After washing with PBS three times, the fluorescent images were imaged by Cytation5 Imaging Reader. The fluorescence statistical analysis of the imaging was performed by imageJ software.

SMMC-7721 cells were fixed with 4 °C methanol (1 min), and then the cell membrane was permeabilized with 1% TritonX-100 (2 min). Afterwards, PBS solution (control experiment), DNase (25 μg/mL) and RNase (25 μg/mL) were added respectively, and incubation was continued for 2 h. Then, the cells were stained with m-lCDs (150 μg/mL, 2 h), Hoechst 33342 (1 μM, 15 min) and SYTO RNASelect (1 μM, 15 min). The fluorescence statistical analysis of the imaging was performed by imageJ software.

In addition, we evaluated the RNA or DNA induced changes in the fluorescence properties by CDs (different molar ratios) and SYTO RNASelect dye. The two dyes (CDs: 150 μg/mL, SYTO RNASelect: 1 μM) were dissolved in aqueous solution (control experiment) or aqueous solution including RNA or DNA (200 μg/mL), respectively, and allowed to fully bind at room temperature. Then, the fluorescence emission spectra (Ex: 488 nm) of each solution were detected with a fluorometer.

Endocytosis inhibition assays were performed to understand the endocytic pathway of CDs. The experiment was divided into six groups, and the first group of cells was incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, to demonstrate the energy dependence of CDs, the second group of cells was incubated at low temperature for 1 h (4 °C) after the addition of CDs. For the other four groups, cells were treated with CPZ (8 μg/mL), filipin (50 μg/mL), EIPA (5 mM), or nocodazole (15 μM) for 2 h (37 °C, 5% CO2), after which CDs (150 μg/mL) were added after washing three times with PBS and continued to incubate for 1 h. The fluorescent images were imaged by Cytation5 Imaging Reader.

The cytotoxicity was performed using the MTT method. SMMC-7721 and LO2 cells were first incubated in 96-well plate for 24 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then CDs (0, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300 or 350 μg/mL; diluted with DMEM) were added and incubated for 24 h or 48 h. Then, 10 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well. After continuing the incubation for 4 h, the supernatant was discarded. Add 100 μL of DMSO solution. The absorbance was measured at 450 nm using the Cytation5 Imaging Reader. The cell viability was estimated according to the following equation:
(1)
V
c e l l
= O
D
T r e a t e d
/ O
D
C o n t r l
×
100
%
Replicate experiments were performed (n = 6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging and characterization of nine CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, nine CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method after cross-linking the nitrogen and carbon sources using glucose, citric acid and m-phenylenediamine as carbon sources and l-aspartic acid, PEI and ammonia as nitrogen sources, respectively. Considering the application of CDs in bioimaging, the cell labeling ability of the above nine CDs was studied. All the CDs could label the cells and exhibit bright fluorescence, as shown in Fig. 1 . The CDs prepared by citric acid as carbon source mainly stained the cytoplasmic regions or the whole regions of the cells (Fig. 1A–C). The CDs obtained with glucose as the carbon source were mainly gathered in the cytoplasmic region (Fig. 1D–F). While the CDs obtained using m-phenylenediamine as the carbon source showed bright fluorescence with high contrast at the nucleolus compared to the perinuclear region, indicating their potential for nucleolus imaging (Fig. 1G–I).

In order to explore the factors affecting the different marking properties of CDs, we further analyzed the surface properties of CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra of these CDs (Figs. S1a–c) have three peaks at ∼250, ∼340 and ∼400 nm. The peak at ∼250 nm is owed to the π-π* leap of the aromatic CC bond, and the peaks at ∼340 and ∼400 nm belong to the n-π* leap of CO and the surface state of CDs [41]. FTIR spectra of the nine CDs (Fig. 2 A–C) show that all samples demonstrated similar peaks in the range of 3400–3200 cm−1 and 1640-1567 cm−1 due to the stretching vibrations of N–H, O–H [27] and CO [42], respectively. Besides, for CDs of c-pCDs, c-aCDs and c-lCDs (Fig. 2A), the peaks at 1456, 1400, 1390 and 1326, 1320, 1324 cm−1 resulted from the CC and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks at 1180, 1222 and 1238 cm−1 resulted from the C–O and C–C stretching vibrations. For CDs of g-pCDs, g-aCDs and g-lCDs (Fig. 2B), the peaks at 1433, 1431 and 1388 cm−1 indicate the N–H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1070, 1071 and 1073 cm−1 resulted from the C–O–C stretching vibrations. For CDs of m-pCDs, m-aCDs and m-lCDs (Fig. 2C), the benzene ring structure occurs at 1500 cm−1 [29,43]. The peaks at 1330, 1332 and 1370 cm−1 suggest that the C–N stretching vibrations [29,44]. The peaks at 1205, 1211 and 1210 cm−1 resulted from the C–O and C–C stretching vibrations [45]. Fig. 2D–F shows the zeta potential of different CDs, where l-aspartic acid is negatively charged for the CDs of nitrogen source. The characterization shows that all CDs contain functional groups such as amino and carboxyl groups, which enhance the hydrophilicity and stability of CDs. However, only the CDs where m-phenylenediamine is the carbon source contain a benzene ring structure, which may be a factor in its targeting of the nucleolus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging and characterization of m-lCDs, o-lCDs and p-lCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the above conjecture, three isomers of phenylenediamine (p-phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, and m-phenylenediamine) containing benzene ring with similar structures were used as carbon sources, and l-aspartic acid was used as nitrogen source to obtain three CDs (p-lCDs, o-lCDs, m-lCDs), respectively. The cell imagings with these three CDs are shown in Fig. 3 . Only the m-lCDs presented obvious nucleolus labeling characteristic, and the o-lCDs and p-lCDs only stained the cytoplasm.

Three CDs were characterized in detail by us. The CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence emission characteristics, and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with increasing excitation wavelength. The m-lCDs, o-lCDs, and p-lCDs showed maximum emission at 520, 570 and 450 nm upon excitation at 440, 420 and 280 nm (Figs. S2a–c), respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra (Figs. S3a and S3b) displayed three peaks at ∼250, ∼340 and ∼400 nm, similar to the above CDs. FTIR spectra of o-lCDs and p-lCDs (Figs. S3c and S3d) showed similar features to that of m-aCDs, m-pCDs and m-lCDs. XPS was used to study the chemical structures and compositions of the CDs. Fig. 4 A–C shows that the CDs mostly consist of carbon (C1s, ∼285.1 eV), nitrogen (N1s, ∼399.1 eV), and oxygen (O1s, ∼532.1 eV), and their atomic percentages are 63.81/59.2/65.17, 14.66/9.08/11.17, 21.3/25.52/22.44 eV, respectively. In the high-resolution curves, the C1s bands of m-lCDs/o-lCDs/p-lCDs (Figs. S4a–c) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.6/285.2/284.5, 286.0/286.5/285.7 and 288.4/288.8/288.1 eV, which can be attributed to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N and CO [25,28], respectively. The O1s bands of m-lCDs/o-lCDs/p-lCDs (Figs. S4d–f) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.2/531.4/531.1, 532.8/533.36/532.8 and 535.3/535.47/535.2 eV, representing CO, C–O, S–O/SO [25,46,47], respectively. The N1s bands of m-lCDs (Fig. 4D) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.9, 400.8 and 401.7 eV, which can be attributed to N–H, pyrrolic N, graphitic N [25,43], respectively. The N1s bands of o-lCDs (Fig. 4E) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.5 and 401.4 eV, representing aromatic nitrogen, C–N–C [27,48], respectively. The N1s bands of p-lCDs (Fig. 4F) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 398.8, 400.2 and 401.2 eV, representing pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen, graphitic nitrogen [28,49], respectively. And zeta potential shows that they are all negatively charged (Fig. S5). This suggests that the targeting of CDs to the nucleolus is closely related to the carbon source. It can be deduced that m-phenylenediamine as the carbon source precursor is the key for CDs to have nucleolus targeting ability, but the benzene ring is not the only factor affecting the CDs properties. From the XPS of the three CDs, we can see that the nitrogen types in o-lCDs are very different from those in m-lCDs/p-lCDs and have low nitrogen content. The m-lCDs contain N–H and pyrrolic N, where the content of pyrrolic N is greater than that in p-lCDs, and it has the highest nitrogen content. Meanwhile, the differences in imaging results suggest that nitrogen content is also a factor in the performance of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging and characterization of m-lCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the effect of nitrogen content, the molar ratio of carbon source (m-phenylenediamine) to nitrogen source (l-aspartic acid) was adjusted to be 1:3, 1:2, 1:1, 1:0.5, 1:0.25, 1:0.125 and 1:0.0625, respectively. For the UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. S6a), it gradually decreased with decreasing nitrogen content. According to the FTIR spectra (Figs. S6b and S6c), the characteristic peaks were almost unaffected by the nitrogen content, which indicates that CDs with different nitrogen content have the same structure.

Their corresponding cell imaging characteristics are exhibited in Fig. 5 A. All the CDs were found to be targeted to the nucleolus upon closer inspection, while the fluorescence intensity was changed versus the nitrogen content. We analyzed the fluorescence intensity of three representative CDs stained cells (1:2, 1:0.5, and 1:0.125). The results showed (Fig. 5B–D) that the highest fluorescence intensity was found at the nucleolus of all cells, and the fluorescence intensity in the nucleolus of cells stained by CDs with ratio of 1:2 and 1:0.5 were approximately 2-fold higher than that of CDs with 1:0.125. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the cytoplasmic region increased to a certain extent with the increase of nitrogen content.

To evaluate the nucleolus targeting effect more accurately, the fluorescence contrast of nucleolus region to cytoplasm area, and the relative fluorescence intensity of the nucleolus region was calculated semiquantitatively by imageJ software. From Fig. 5E and F, it can be seen more visually that the fluorescence in the nucleolus region increases with increasing nitrogen source. CDs with a molar ratio of 1:0.5 had the highest fluorescence contrast in the nucleolus and cytoplasmic regions, the optimal nucleolus labeling effect was obtained, which was with the excellent fluorescent intensity and significant contrast between nucleolus and cytoplasm area. It was more clearly shown that the fluorescence intensity at the nucleolus increased with increasing nitrogen content, and the staining effect was best at 1:0.5.

In view of the good imaging effect of m-lCDs with a molar ratio of 1:0.5, their morphology was were further characterized as representative. TEM images (Fig. S7) showed that the m-lCDs were almost spherical and well dispersed with an average diameter of about 4.4 nm. Meanwhile, they have a graphite-like crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm [28,37]. The influence of nitrogen content on the imaging effect of o-lCDs and p-lCDs were also explored. However, neither o-lCDs nor p-lCDs with different nitrogen contents were able to stain the nuclei of the cells (Fig. S8). It further indicates that m-phenylenediamine as a carbon source is the key to nucleolus imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specific targeting of RNA by m-lCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Nucleolus targeting mechanism of CDs studied by digestion experiments, and the results of CDs were compared with that of the commercial RNA (SYTO RNASelect) and DNA dyes (Hoechst 33342). As shown in Fig. 6 A, after RNase treatment, m-lCDs and SYTO RNASelect were uniformly distributed throughout the cells. The fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced, and the labeling properties of the nucleolus were also lost. In contrast, after treatment with DNase the staining properties of m-lCDs and SYTO RNASelect did not change significantly and the nucleolus showed bright fluorescence. For the Hoechst 33342 dye, the fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced only after DNase digestion treatment. It shows that the CDs are bound to the RNA in the cell. In addition, the cytotoxicity of m-lCDs to SMMC-7721 and LO2 cells was negligible (Figs. S9a and S9b), indicating that they can be used in the biomedical field. Meanwhile, the m-lCDs can also clearly label nucleoli in live cells, as seen in Fig. S10.

The response of CDs with different nitrogen content to RNA or DNA was further investigated. The fluorescence intensity of CDs, SYTO RNASelect in RNA or DNA solutions was tested. As Fig. 6B–I, the fluorescence intensity of CDs with molar ratios of 1:3, 1:2, 1:1, 1:0.5, and 1:0.25 was significantly stronger in RNA solution than in aqueous solution and DNA solution, especially the fluorescence intensity of CDs with molar ratio of 1:0.5 in RNA solution was as high as about 5 times that in aqueous solution. CDs with molar ratios of 1:0.125 and 1:0.0625 showed essentially the same fluorescence intensity in RNA and DNA solutions, this suggests that as the nitrogen content in the CDs decreases, their ability to target the nucleolus progressively decreases. Also the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases with the decrease of nitrogen content, same as imaging results (Fig. 5A).

To understand the intracellular transport mechanism of the nucleolus targeting m-lCDs, the cellular uptake pathways were explored in the existence of multiple endocytosis inhibitors. We evaluated temperature (4 °C) and four cytosolic inhibitors on the cellular uptake of CDs.

From Fig. 7 A and B, it can be observed that the nucleolus targeting ability of CDs was almost unaffected by these five factors. Compared to the fluorescence of the untreated group, the fluorescence of the 4 °C group was obviously decreased by 75%. This phenomenon may be due to the fact that at low temperatures ATP-generating enzymes are less active, indicating that low temperature significantly blocked the cellular internalization of CDs. For the experimental groups using the four cytosolic inhibitors, the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the EIPA and filipin groups decreased significantly by 60% and 47%, respectively, compared to the CDs group. Nocod. group showed a slight decrease of 25%, and the CPZ group remained almost unchanged. This indicates that the pathways involved in the endocytosis of CDs are mainly temperature-dependent transport and micropinocytosis, caveolin and microtubules are also involved in the endocytosis of CDs [28].

In summary, a range of CDs were prepared by selecting different precursors and changing the nitrogen source content, and it was found that carbon source and nitrogen content contributed significantly to the selective nucleolus staining and imaging of CDs. It is mainly manifested in the following points: the key to preparing nucleolus-targeted CDs is to use m-phenylenediamine as the carbon source; the nitrogen content also affects the performance of CDs. As the nitrogen content decreases, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreases, and the nucleolus targeting performance of CDs also reduces. When the molar ratio of carbon source to nitrogen source is 1:0.5, the prepared CDs have satisfactory nucleolus targeting properties and imaging effect. It was fully demonstrated by cell digestion experiments that CDs bind to RNA in the nucleolus. The prepared m-lCDs have good specificity, biocompatibility, high nucleolus targeting and clear nucleolus imaging effect. Therefore, they can be used as cell nucleolus imaging probes. This study investigates the factors affecting the performance of CDs and provides ideas and methods for the design of nucleolus-targeted CDs.

Qianqian Duan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Zhiwen Guan: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Juanjuan Xue: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Boye Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology. Lan Zhou: Funding acquisition. Muyue Jia: Formal analysis. Si Si: Formal analysis. Shengbo Sang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51975400, 51622507, 51505324), Shanxi Provincial Key Medical Scientific Research Project (2021XM12), Open Fund from the Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology (Shanxi Medical University) (No. KLMEC/SXMU202001), Shanxi “1331 Project” Key Subjects Construction (1331KSC).
